figures.caption: Figure 1: Editing results across NTI [14], NPI [13], and our ResetEdit. Under the same editing prompts, NTI and NPI fail to faithfully realize the requested changes or introduce unwanted alterations, whereas ResetEdit reconstructs a reliable starting latent that enables accurate and localized edits.

Table 1: Quantitative comparison of different inversion methods combined with three representative tuning-free editing pipelines (P2P, PnP, and MasaCtrl) on PIE-Bench and ImageNet-R-TI2I. Results are reported in terms of CLIP Score and CLIP Similarity (↑ indicates higher is better). ResetEdit consistently achieves the best performance across all pipelines and benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness in reconstructing a reliable starting latent for high-fidelity editing.

Table 2: Results on PIE-Bench across three representative editing pipelines: from top to bottom, (a) P2P, (b) PnP, and (c) MasaCtrl. Each entry reports CLIP Score / CLIP Directional Similarity for all sub-categories. Inv. denotes inversion-based methods, and attr. abbreviates attribute. The best results are highlighted in bold, and the second-best is underlined.
Figure 4: Qualitative comparison of different inversion methods combined with P2P on ImageNet-R-TI2I and PIE-Bench.
Figure 5: Qualitative comparison of different inversion methods combined with PnP on ImageNet-R-TI2I and PIE-Bench.
Figure 6: Qualitative comparison of different inversion methods combined with MasaCtrl on ImageNet-R-TI2I and PIE-Bench.

Figure 2: Comparison between DDIM inversion-based editing (top) and ResetEdit (bottom). In the DDIM-based family, NTI, NPI, and ProxNPI primarily mitigate Condition Drift, while EDICT, DDPM inversion, and Direct Inversion primarily aim to reduce Estimation Error. However, all remain constrained by the asymmetry between forward and backward processes. In contrast, ResetEdit proactively embeds the discrepancy during generation and later extracts it to reconstruct a faithful starting latent, providing a reliable anchor for precise and semantically aligned editing.

Figure 8: Effect of VAE optimization under the P2P [5] framework. From left to right: source image, edited result using the starting latent without VAE optimization, and edited result using the starting latent with VAE optimization.

Figure 7: Effect of residual injection on image generation quality. From left to right: image generated without residual injection, image with injected residual, and their corresponding difference map. The average MSE is 8.47 × 10−5, with a PSNR of 40.72 dB, indicating high visual fidelity.

Figure 3: The framework of our proposed ResetEdit, which comprises two key modules: Residual Compression and Reconstruction Modules (RCM and RRM), and Residual Injection and Extraction Modules (RIM and REM). During image generation, RCM compresses the latent discrepancy, which is then injected by RIM. In the editing phase, REM first extracts the embedded residual, and RRM reconstructs an accurate starting latent for subsequent precise image editing.
